Can you recall a moment when everything was going wrong around you — a project falling apart, a cancelled ride, or a total surprise — but against all odds, you managed to stay calm? What was going through your mind at that exact moment?
What was the most frustrating moment last month when you stubbornly fought against something you absolutely could not change? Tell us about this battle against windmills.
Have you ever had to make a very unpopular or costly decision simply because you felt it was 'the right thing' to do? What was the turning point and how did it end?
We all have a memory of a situation where we didn't act according to our values out of fear or convenience. Could you tell us about that event and how you felt afterwards?

Can you tell us about an event that felt like a catastrophe at the time, but in hindsight turned out to be the starting point of something positive? What happened between the moment of 'drama' and the moment of realization?
Have you ever been absolutely furious at someone, only to later discover a detail that radically changed your view of the situation? Tell us about that tipping point when your anger vanished.
What was the most embarrassing or difficult moment of your week? In retrospect, how much was 'the event itself' and how much was 'the judgment' you placed on it?
Has there been a moment in your life where you felt 'completely present', without any thought of yesterday or tomorrow? What about that event anchored you in the present?
Can you tell us about a specific hour of your week when you felt like time had stopped? What was happening around you?
Do you remember a moment when you noticed something — a sensation, an emotion, a thought — that you wouldn't normally have noticed? What happened?
Have you ever had an experience — even a brief one — where you felt deeply at peace, or completely present? What was going on at that moment?
Can you recall a moment when you would have normally reacted impulsively, but didn't? What happened inside?
Has there been a moment in your life when you suddenly saw a familiar situation in a completely different light? What had changed?
Have you ever experienced a moment when doing the right thing was clear, but difficult? What happened?
Can you recall a moment when something someone told you — or that you read, saw, or lived through — struck you as an obvious truth you had never been able to put into words? What was it?
Can you think of a moment when something you believed to be permanent — a relationship, a certainty, an identity — turned out to be fragile or temporary? How did you experience that?
Has there been a moment when the loss of something opened you up to something else? What happened?
Do you remember a difficult period that eventually taught you something important? What allowed you to get through it?
Has your body ever taught you something your mind hadn't yet understood? What happened?
Have you ever experienced a moment of silence — chosen or imposed — that surprised you by what it contained? What did you find there?
Do you remember a moment when you kept doing something — a practice, an effort, a commitment — without knowing if it was worth it? What kept you going?
Has there been a moment when repeating something over and over — a gesture, a habit, a ritual — revealed something to you that you wouldn't have seen otherwise?
Have you ever felt that time was stretching or contracting — that a few minutes lasted an eternity, or that an hour passed in an instant? In what context?
Has there been a moment when you realized you had been telling yourself a false story about yourself or about a situation? What allowed you to see things differently?
Can you recall a moment when you felt compassion for someone — or for yourself — in an unexpected way? What happened?
Do you remember a moment when you felt deeply aligned with your values — even if it cost you something? How did you experience that afterward?
Have you ever gone through something difficult whose meaning only became clear later — sometimes much later? What allowed you to make that connection?
Has there been a moment of solitude — physical or inner — that taught you something about yourself that the company of others would have hidden from you?
If you had to choose one experience in your life that taught you the most about the nature of the mind — or about what it means to be human — which would you choose? What happened?
Tell me about a recent moment with someone when you were reachable but not truly available — physically there, but elsewhere inside. What kept you from really being with that person?
Describe a moment when someone was truly available to you. What, concretely, made you feel that (a gesture, a silence, a quality of attention, etc.)?
With whom in your life do you most often feel available? And what does that person do that makes it possible?

The welcoming silence — the one that lets the other person go all the way to the end of what they have to say — is rarer. What pushes you to fill others' silences with advice, anecdotes about your own life, and so on?
Think of a relationship in which you feel good, one that involves no calculation or strategy. What does it nourish in you that no new encounter could ever replace?

Rosa speaks of the "logic of the option" applied to relationships: always being able to change, to leave, to find something better elsewhere. In which relationships do you feel this tension between staying and exploring elsewhere?
A relationship as soil rather than investment — what does that concretely change in the way you tend to it day to day? Give an example from your own life.
We often ask "how are you?" without really wanting to know, and answer "fine" without really saying anything. What would help you break out of this automatism in your own conversations?
With whom in your life can you tell the story rather than summarize what's happening to you? What does that person do that makes the storytelling possible?
Describe a moment shared recently where you searched for words together, without knowing in advance where it would lead. And what threatens this kind of moment in your current relationships?
At what precise moment do you sense a relationship tipping toward something you use or optimize? What are the signs, for you?
Have you ever been consumed by someone — used as an audience, as support, as distraction? And conversely: have you ever realized that you were consuming a relationship?
"Useful" friendships, professional contacts, ties of circumstance: at what point can they become resonant, and at what point do they remain mere consumption?
Messaging apps, social networks, and dating apps transform the way we connect. In your own relationships, where do you see the line between what nourishes resonance and what slips into consumption?
If you had to identify a single practice to protect or establish in order to stay available to those who matter to you, which would it be, and what would be the conditions for cultivating it?
